Now we're going to talk about, you'll be happy to know, we're going to get to the actual writing of copy, writing a promotion, writing a letter, writing an ad, writing of whatever it is. And the main thing I think is to not think so much of getting words down on paper, and you've done this a little bit already. You've written a lot. You've written stories. You've written about your prospect. You can see where good copy comes from. So now what we want to do, we're going to look at what copy is, right? So it's, you know, there's a headline, you know, you've got a subhead maybe, deer, whoever, and then, you know, a lead, right, that comes off of the headline. And then you've got the close at the end, right, C-L-O-S-E, where you talk about the guarantee, you talk about the bonuses, you talk about the offer. What is it exactly you're offering? All this stuff in here is what we've talked about in a way. It's bullets. It's talking, we haven't talked about bullets yet, but it's stories. It's talking about the prospect, you know, about their life, what the product can do for them. And it's some things that we'll also talk about, which is bullets, right? And that's going to be the main thing we're going to work on first. What I wanted to get across about the letter is it's not so much what I drew here. It's not so much going from here down to there like that, like, oh, I got to write this, I got to write this, I got to write this, now I got to write, oh, now it's time to write this. It's really more like this. It's like, here you've got this prospect, and he's going through his life, and he's relatively happy, he's got things to do, he's not interested in your prospect, in your product. And you want to take him on a journey to get to here, which is, you know, I'm so bad at drawing, but now he's got your product. Now he's got your newsletter, right? And how do we go from here to here, right? And there's two journeys that we take the prospect on. One journey is kind of an intellectual journey, from head to head, right, from the logic of it. Here are the reasons why. Here are the things it can do for you. Here's other people who have done it. It's very, very logical, right, that part of the journey. But what a lot of people don't realize in copying, if you really look at copy, sometimes you'll see it doesn't have this. That's the journey down here, which is the emotional journey, right? So that's like, like we talked before, that's from his heart, from your heart to his heart, or her heart, right from there, to go like that. The prospect starts out here, and they're not interested, they're not feeling anything. They're not even feeling unhappy, they're not feeling dissatisfied, they're not feeling their pain, they're just sort of feeling like, you know, I'm just going through my day, and here's this thing on my computer screen, here's this thing in my mail. And so you want to take them on a journey from indifference to interest. You want to get them interested. You want to get them to feel the pain. You want to get them to feel some emotion, like we keep talking about the grandmothers fighting and playing with your grandkids. You want to take them on that emotional journey of longing and of things that they don't want, and being afraid that things that they don't want will happen to them. And then finally, that emotional journey, as you start to get here, the emotional journey is one now of desire. Oh, if only I had this information. Oh, if only I had this product, this newsletter, whatever it is, that can give me this information. I want it. I feel it. That's an emotional, emotional thing. It's not not from the head. It's a feeling. So when you write copy, when you look at your own copy, when a copywriter gives copy to you, always make sure that there's the logical emotional journey. Right, does it make sense? Is it logical? Oh, if this is true, if I need this kind of information to do what I want, then I must need this newsletter, because this newsletter gives me that information, that enables me to do what I want. Right? Is it all logical? Does it all follow? And then, is there the emotional journey? Are we making the prospect feel anything along the way? Or is it only, is it only up here, which is so empty and so boring and so incomplete when it comes to copywriting? And when you read great copywriting, when you read good letters, and I hope maybe the best writers every day study promotions that are successful. Joe Schrieffer at Agora says, you know, like vitamins, every day you should read a promotion if you work for him. And it's such a good thing to do, because it gets good copy into your head. So when you do that, notice how the good copywriters, the great copywriters, take the reader on an emotional journey, as well as an intellectual journey. So resistance, right? You push against me, I push against you. You try to sell me something, I try to not let you sell you something, right? You go into a store and the guy says, can I help you? You know, like, no, I'm just looking, I'm just looking, don't try to sell me anything. And as soon as someone starts selling, you meet them with resistance. Oh, I don't know if you're believable, I don't know if I can believe you, that sounds like a big claim. I don't know, you know. And in End of America, they didn't never pushed against you with that kind of selling. So there was never this resistance. So they could convince you and convince you and bring you to this. Remember, we saw that line to go from here to here. It got you to that place, because you didn't push against it. Gene Schwartz has a thing about there being five stages of sophistication of a market. And it's great to look at your own market and look at advertising like this. You know, it starts out where you're like in stage one, it's like you're the only one, let's say. Probably none of that, maybe some of us have had that experience in business to business. But it's like, you're like, let's say weight loss, right? The first weight loss product ever. All they had to say was lose weight. I say, oh, okay, I'll buy that. I want to lose weight. You know, that's great. Then there were others, right? Other people come along. And they say, no, no, lose weight. We'll help you lose weight. No, we'll help you lose weight. So now you have to say, you have to make your claim bigger, right? You have to say, lose 20 pounds, lose 10 pounds in a week. The claims get bigger and bigger until there's all these people making all these claims, too many claims. So what do you do then? You add something called a mechanism. It's how you explain how you do something, right? So now it's lose weight, but lose weight by rubbing your tummy away. Lose weight while you sleep. Lose weight without dieting. Lose weight thanks to this miracle pill from the Amazon. You know, lose weight thanks to this miracle machine that you just hook it up around your waist and it vibrates and it makes the weight shake right off. Lose weight with this miracle drink, which makes your metabolism go faster, raises your metabolism and makes you lose weight. And then four, you got too many, this is too many claims, and then too many mechanisms. And so this is where I think a lot of things are right now, like in investing. You know, there's like, we have this great mechanism for investing. We have this great way of investing. We have four people. That's our mechanism. You know, we have this, we have something that was founded. Our guru comes from a certain place, has a certain technique. So then everybody tries to get a better and better mechanism. And that's what you see now in weight loss. You know, lose weight with this herb from the Amazon, crazy kind of stuff. And then finally, which is where weight loss is now, and also maybe where investing is, you know, it's called saturation. There's so much out there that people don't believe anymore. And so you can't just make a claim. You can't just make a, you can't make a, just talk about a mechanism because no one believes anything anymore. No one believes you can lose weight. No one believes you can investing. I don't know who to believe. There's so much noise out there. So what you do then, you got to come at it more indirectly. And that's when you do things like, like really clever ideas like why French women never get fat. You ever hear of that book? Why French women never get fat? It's a great book. It's a great title, even if I don't really know anything about it. But I just, the idea that French women never get fat, well, yeah, that makes sense. When you see French women, they're always so skinny. They're known for being skinny. And yet French people are known for eating a lot of food and eating a lot of heavy sauces, drinking a lot of wine. So why is it they never get fat? So that's not a claim. I don't resist it now. That's not a mechanism. You know, it's a whole new way of looking at it. Another way in when you're at stage five is to, and that's a lot of what we've been doing is to kind of address the prospect. Like if you've tried everything to lose weight and failed, I can tell you why. And I can tell you what will work. So you see that how that kind of acknowledges the fact that there's so much out there and there's disbelief now. This is such a powerful way of thinking. Because if you look at ads maybe from your competitors, if you look at ads from other people, and you really bear this in mind, a lot of times you can see, well, that won't work. That's, you know, that's too far back from where the market is now. The market is gone to here. Just like maybe the market has progressed beyond the end of America. They've heard that argument, oh, the world is coming to an end. Now they need something different.
